html:
Chaowei Power is not the clean “net cash, 1x earnings” story in the prior draft. It is better, and riskier, than that: a hugely scaled Chinese lead-acid motive battery and recycling platform trading around HKD1.74, or roughly HKD1.9 to 2.0 billion of market value, against RMB54.4 billion of 2025 revenue, RMB361 million of owner net profit, and RMB6.65 billion of book equity. That is about 4.8x trailing earnings and 0.27x book, with a near-term HKD0.056 dividend going ex-dividend on June 5, 2026 and payable July 15.

The core variant view is that the market has labeled Chaowei as obsolete lead-acid hardware just as China policy has become more supportive of safer, compliant e-bike replacement demand. China’s new e-bike safety standard took effect September 1, 2025, and government trade-in programs sold millions of replacement e-bikes in 2025. More important, the policy specifically allows stronger support for consumers trading old lithium-ion e-bikes into lead-acid models. That is the opposite of the “forced lithium extinction” thesis.

The 2025 filing already shows the inflection. Electric bike battery revenue rose to RMB19.96 billion from RMB18.48 billion. Lead-acid motive batteries overall were RMB28.82 billion, 53% of revenue. Renewable materials, essentially the recycling and materials loop, grew to RMB24.96 billion and is now 46% of revenue, though lower margin. Lithium-ion revenue more than doubled to RMB625 million from RMB264 million, still tiny in mix but large enough to matter if quality improves. Chaowei also launched sodium-battery products in 2025 and began a hard-carbon anode materials project with a stated 30,000-ton annual output target at full production.

The upside torque is in margins, not sales growth. Gross margin was only 6.7% in 2025. On RMB54 billion of revenue, a 50 bp improvement in gross margin is RMB272 million of incremental gross profit, almost 75% of reported owner net income. A mix shift away from low-margin renewable materials, better pricing under the new standard, fewer credit losses, or reduced R&D drag can move earnings far faster than revenue. The company spent RMB1.41 billion on R&D in 2025, nearly four times owner profit. That is either waste, or a very large embedded call option if sodium, lithium, graphene-enhanced lead-acid, or energy storage products begin to monetize.

This is not a net-cash thesis. Chaowei had RMB9.87 billion of borrowings at year-end 2025, RMB3.75 billion of cash, and RMB3.36 billion of restricted bank deposits. Management’s own net debt figure, including restricted deposits, was RMB2.76 billion. That keeps EV-based valuation from looking as absurdly cheap as the P/E and P/B do. It also means the buyback story should be treated cautiously: the April circular seeks authority to repurchase up to 10% of shares, but the company had not repurchased shares in the previous six months. The real capital-return evidence is the dividend increase, not an active buyback.

The market is ignoring Chaowei because it sits in an ugly category: Chinese small cap, lead exposure, thin institutional ownership, founder control, low margins, and heavy working-capital debt. Retail/social context reinforces the discount. English e-bike discussions mostly treat lead-acid as outdated, while Chinese social media has circulated labor and lead-exposure allegations around a Guangxi “Chaowei Battery” plant. Whether or not that specific allegation becomes material, it captures the ESG discount that will always attach to the company.

The base case is still attractive because the stock does not need hero assumptions. If 2026 owner earnings rise to RMB450 to 500 million as trade-in demand continues, lithium/sodium losses stay contained, and gross margin stabilizes, EPS is roughly HKD0.44 to 0.49. A 6.5x multiple puts the stock around HKD3.00 to 3.20, still below 0.5x book. That is not an aggressive valuation for a national leader whose main end market just received policy support.

The bull case is a multiple and earnings reset. If gross margin recovers 70 to 90 bps, credit losses normalize, and new battery products stop being seen as a cash drain, owner earnings can move toward RMB650 to 750 million. At 7 to 8x earnings, or around 0.75x book, the stock can trade above HKD5.00. The market does not need to believe Chaowei is CATL. It only needs to stop valuing it like a dying recycler with no terminal equity value.

The bear case is also clear. If lead-acid demand rolls over despite the policy support, renewable-materials mix keeps depressing margins, R&D remains a sinkhole, and environmental scrutiny rises, earnings can fall back toward RMB250 million. With debt and governance concerns still unresolved, the market could value that at 4 to 5x earnings, or roughly HKD1.10 to 1.30.

Bear target: HKD1.20, down 31% from HKD1.74.

Base target: HKD3.20, up 84% from HKD1.74.

Bull target: HKD5.20, up 199% from HKD1.74.
